1

POWERLIFTING

3

What is powerlifting?
Powerlifting is a strength sport in which participants try to lift the most weight in the 3
powerlifts: the parallel barbell back squat, the paused barbell bench press and the
barbell deadlift from the floor, typically in this order over the course of the competition.
Each participant has 3 attempts for each powerlift with rest in between each attempt to
perform the highest 1RM. The total sum of the highest 1RM in each powerlift is then
normalized by the lifter's bodyweight in a complex formula called the IPF Formula (a
replacement of the older Wilks Formula) to calculate who has the most strength relative
to his or her bodyweight. The lifter with the most IPF Points wins.

Federations & rules
The International Powerlifting Federation (IPF) is the largest and arguably the most
prestigious powerlifting federation with the highest prize pools. Many other federations
use their rule set, so if you have any interest in competing, you should familiarize
yourself with the IPF powerlifting ruleset.

There are many other powerlifting federations [2, 3], however, and most local level
lifters probably decide which show to go to primarily based on its geographical
proximity. Each competition can have different rules, so make sure to study the
applicable ruleset before you register.

There are 2 key differences between different powerlifting competitions.
1. Drug testing. The IPF is drug-tested. Some competitions are not. Note that there
is no such thing as a drug-free federation, however. The use of performance
enhancing drugs is very common in high level powerlifting and you could even
say it's a practical requirement to compete internationally. Some federations are
just stricter with testing than others.

4

2. Raw vs. Equipped. Equipped/Geared powerlifting is performed in an extremely
tight supportive lifting suit consisting of single- or even multiple-ply polyester that
majorly increases how much you can lift. Equipped competitions typically allow
participants to wear knee and elbow wraps, a weightlifting belt, a bench press
shirt, a squat suit or a deadlift suit. Raw/Classic competitions only allow knee and
elbow sleeves, a weightlifting belt and wrist wraps. In this module we'll focus on
Classic/Raw powerlifting, not Equipped/Geared powerlifting. Preparation for an
Equipped competition is in principle identical to a Raw competition, except that
you should train in the suits and find the strongest allowed suits so you get the
most assistance. In any show, you should wear as much supportive training gear
as is allowed, and you should train the powerlifts using this gear in preparation
for the competition.

Equipped powerlifting

Am I strong enough to compete?
If you're interested in powerlifting, you're probably interested in having some frame of
reference of what constitutes competitive powerlifting strength. The following table
headings: Am I strong enough to compete?

pages.text:
5

summarizes competition data from the USA Powerlifting Federation, which is drug-
tested, from 2012-2016 in Raw powerlifting.

Now let's go into how to get this strong. The goal of a powerlifting program is to
maximize strength development in the powerlifts. As you've learned how to program for
strength and how to perform the 3 powerlifts - the barbell squat, the deadlift and the
barbell bench press - in principle you have the tools to design effective powerlifting
programs. Winning a powerlifting competition, however, requires a bit more knowledge
and planning. In this module we'll briefly revisit each important programming aspect for
powerlifting and discuss what goes into winning a powerlifting competition beyond just
being strong.

Let's start by recapping what you've learned about programming for maximum strength
development.

6

Understanding strength
Powerlifting is not about being the strongest lifter. You can be an amazing sprinter, pole
vaulter, Olympic Weightlifter and gymnast and you can hold world records in the front
squat, the barbell overhead press and the Romanian deadlift, it doesn't matter for
powerlifting. It doesn't even matter if you hold the record in the bench-225-for-reps
American football test. The only thing that matters for powerlifting is what your 1RM is in
the bench, back-squat and deadlift.

While muscle size is in principle irrelevant for powerlifting, muscle size is vital for muscle
strength in the long run. As you've learned, you can think of strength as the speed of a
race car: you can only achieve maximum speed with a big engine (muscle hypertrophy)
and a skilled driver (neuromuscular efficiency). So a powerlifter must check all the
boxes a bodybuilder must for maximum muscle hypertrophy, but he also has to
specialize in 1RM strength development of the big 3 powerlifts.

And herein lies what is arguably the most fundamental difference between training for
size and powerlifting: specificity. The goal of powerlifting is much more specific, so the
training must be as well.

7

Exercise selection
Let's start with the obvious one: powerlifters have to train the powerlifts. In fact, in
theory powerlifters could probably do nothing but the powerlifts, as they are the most
specific training possible. However, in practice doing nothing but high-volume, high-
frequency, high-intensity powerlifting will quickly cause overuse injuries in most lifters,
so it's prudent to spend some training volume on accessory lifts. Training other
movement patterns, especially for the back muscles, can also help prevent extreme
structural imbalances and help develop muscle mass more effectively than the
powerlifts, which can over the long run increase powerlifting strength.

As such, the exercise selection for powerlifters can be categorized into 3 groups.
1. The powerlifts. These are the foundation of your program.
2. Accessory lifts that are expected to have acute performance carry-over to the
powerlifts. While almost all exercises will have some degree of carry-over, this
category principally includes variants of the powerlifts, such as front squats,
dumbbell bench presses and Romanian deadlifts. The main goal of these
exercises is to get effective training volume in with lower overuse risk than
performing more of the powerlifts.
3. Mass builders. These exercises make up your remaining training volume for
maximum muscle growth to make your muscles bigger and your connective
tissues happier.

The following are great accessory exercises for the powerlifts that most powerlifters
should incorporate over time. You should in particular incorporate accessory exercises
that aid you in your sticking point for each powerlift, e.g. if your sticking point in the
deadlift is right on the floor, deficit deadlifts will likely help particularly well, whereas if
your sticking point is at the top, Romanian deadlifts and hip thrusts will probably help
more.

8

• Squats: full range of motion (ROM) high bar back squats, front squats, squats
with bands or chains, Goodmornings with your squat stance, belt squats,
Bulgarian split squats.
• Deadlifts: Snatch-grip or deficit deadlifts with your competition stance (Snatch-
grip only possible for conventional stance), Romanian deadlifts, deadlifts with
chains or bands, barbell shrugs, trap bar deadlifts.
• Bench: dumbbell bench presses, bench presses with bands or chains and a
bodybuilding grip (closer grip to maximize ROM), skull-overs, push-ups (for
shoulder health), barbell overhead presses (or incline presses), specialty bar
bench presses, decline barbell bench presses.

If your grip is a limiting factor for deadlifts, meaning you can lift more with straps than
without, you'll also have to program in a lot of grip work for the forearms, assuming you
compete in a show that doesn't allow weightlifting straps. Use chalk and a mixed grip for
your deadlifts if you can't hold an overhand grip. In principle the forearms can be trained
like any other muscle with the same volume etc., but the carry-over of different types of
forearm strength is small, so focus most of your training on static holds. A very time-
efficient way to add grip work to any program is to hold the bar until grip failure after the
last set of any deadlift, shrug or pull-up exercise. If this doesn't suffice, add more sets of
static holds. Be sure to monitor the time of each hold and implement progressive
overload just like for any other exercise, e.g. progressively overload the weight you can
hold for a maximum of 15 seconds.

Let's discuss how much of each category you should perform in your training.

9

Training volume
The total number of work sets per muscle group in a powerlifting program should ideally
be the same as for maximum muscle growth, since maximum muscle growth is needed
for maximum strength development over the long run.

Acutely however, you've seen in our in-house meta-analysis of the literature that
strength gains are less sensitive to training volume per muscle group than muscle
growth is. For trained individuals, as all powerlifters should be, the dose-response is
reasonably strong up to 10 sets per week, suggestive up to 20 sets per week, but after
that, the relation becomes too faint and unreliable that it's hard to define an optimum at
even the population level. The weaker effect of training volume on strength than muscle
growth makes perfect sense, as for muscle growth, the total number of sets for that
muscle group represents a direct measure of the stimulus for adaptation (mechanical
tension to stimulate muscle hypertrophy). Strength gains, however, are exercise-
specific, so the total number of sets for the chest may not represent the effective
training stimulus for barbell bench press strength. The barbell bench press will have
higher carry-over than a dumbbell bench press, for example. The contribution of an
exercise to strength development in the powerlifts can also be affected by any of the
other factors discussed in this module, notably training intensity. Overall, we can at least
say that most powerlifters should ideally do at least 10-20 sets per week of exercises
that are expected to have any direct carry-over to the powerlifts. This includes the
powerlifts themselves, any roughly similar compound exercises and isolation exercises
for the prime movers of the powerlifts, such as chest flys for the bench press. Other
isolation exercises, such as calf raises and cable lateral raises, do not count towards
this target, only towards the total set volume per muscle group.

For powerlifting the most important measure of training volume is arguably the total
number of weekly sets for each powerlift. A meta-analysis by Ralston et al. (2017)
studied the effect of training volume per exercise on strength development of that
exercise. The data showed a significant advantage of 10+ sets per exercise per week

10

over 4 or fewer sets, albeit with strong diminishing returns. Limited data indicated 5-9
sets also outperformed 4 or fewer sets. There were not enough data to compare 10+
sets vs. 5-9 sets, but the average effect size in the total analysis of all exercises
grouped together suggested a marginal advantage in favor of 10+ sets. Thus, we can
say that 5 sets per week of each powerlift generally constitute a minimum for maximum
strength development and benefits may continue above 10 sets.

There's only 1 study that has compared a set training volume of 5+ to 10+ in a powerlift
without confounding training from other exercises in trained individuals. Marshall et al.
(2011) studied the effect of squatting twice per week for 1, 4 or 8 sets per workout while
doing no other lower body training. The results indicated 16 weekly sets of squatting
outperformed 2 sets. No other between-group comparisons reached statistical
significance, but if we look at the data below, we can see that there was a dose-
response trend with the highest strength development observed in the 16-set group.

11

Squat strength development in trained men doing nothing but squats for the lower body,
2x per week for a total of 2, 8 or 16 sets of squats. Source

In sum, powerlifters should ideally train each muscle group with the same set volume as
that for maximum muscle growth, as muscle growth is required for maximal long-term
strength development. This volume should generally include 10-20 sets of exercises
that are expected to have at least some acute carry-over to the powerlifts. The
powerlifts themselves should be trained with at least 5 sets per week each and
generally 8-16 sets each, recovery permitting.

Now let's discuss how we should distribute this training volume across the week.

12

Training frequency
The potential benefits of higher training frequencies for muscle growth also apply to
strength development.
• You can perform more work, produce more force and reach higher levels of
muscle activation when a muscle hasn't accumulated neuromuscular fatigue yet.
All of these factors should be beneficial for maximal strength development.
• Recovery capacity is better with higher training frequencies, at least if we go by
muscle soreness, active recovery research, the T/C-ratio and injury rates.

If we look exclusively at training frequency studies on competitive strength athletes, 3
out of 3 studies find higher training frequencies improve strength development
independent of weekly set volume.
• The Norwegian Frequency Project found greater strength development when a
3-day full-body program was split into 6 days in national level powerlifters.
• Hartman et al. (2007), Häkkinen & Pakarinen (1991) and Häkkinen & Kallinen
(1994) found greater strength development in female athletes, nationally
competitive male weightlifters and male powerlifters and bodybuilders when
performing their workouts spread over 2 daily sessions instead of 1.

Based on these results, powerlifters can generally train exclusively with full-body
workouts. Moreover, the powerlifts or close variants should be trained at least 2x per
week and preferably 3x or more. Anecdotally, the squat lends itself the best to higher
training frequencies (3x or more), followed by the bench press (2x or more), whereas
the deadlift can be problematic to train more than even once per week, as it can really
beat you up. In practice, the problem with training the powerlifts often is generally not
muscular recovery but connective tissue damage and subsequent injury risk, so
powerlifters should find the highest frequency they tolerate for each powerlift.

13

Training intensity
The programming of training intensity represents a key difference between a
powerlifting and a bodybuilding type program. Whereas for muscle growth you can
achieve good results anywhere between 30-90% of 1RM, for powerlifting it's almost
essential to go heavy: most training of the powerlifts should be done in the 1-5 rep
range with weights at or above 80% of 1RM to maximize strength development.

True 1RMs should be performed with some regularity, both for strength development
and because it's the most specific training possible for powerlifters. It's crucial to have a
good idea of your 1RM in each powerlift before a competition so you can select weights
that amount to a true 1RM. We'll get back to how to select weights optimally later.

Accessory lifts with high expected carry-over to the powerlifts, such as front squats and
dumbbell bench presses, should ideally also be trained heavy for maximum
performance transfer. Accessory lifts should have high carry-over if they're multi-
joint/compound exercises with a similar movement pattern to one of the powerlifts.

Tertiary exercises and most single-joint/isolation exercises can be trained relatively
light. Having high reps in the program may benefit muscle growth by emphasizing type I
muscle fibers and different growth pathways. More importantly in practice, high reps are
more joint-friendly and, as we'll discuss below, injuries represent a very common and
serious obstacle for powerlifters. Individuals with weaker connective tissues are often
forced to use higher reps for most of their training outside the powerlifts, simply because
the high volume of high intensity training in barbell exercises is already problematic to
the extent that compounding further low-rep training on that is impossible without
frequent injuries.

14

Exercise technique
The technique of accessory and mass building exercises in a powerlifting program can
be performed as in a bodybuilding type program. The powerlifts, however, must be
performed in a specific manner according to the ruleset of the competition. Within these
constraints, the best technique for each lift is the one you're strongest with, so
experiment with different techniques. Below are general guidelines for how to find the
technique that's best for you.

The bench press
The bench press has to be performed with a pause with the bar on the chest until the
'press' signal is given.

The goal of powerlifting is to lift as much as possible, not to stimulate the pecs, delts or
triceps optimally for muscle growth. This means you should find the technique that
allows you to lift as much as possible. Since moving any weight over a longer distance
is harder, you should shorten the range of motion (ROM) as much as possible. You
generally achieve this with the widest grip that's allowed and as much arch in your back
as possible to push your chest up without your butt coming off the bench.

Good powerlifting bench press technique. The closer
you can bring your chest to the bar, the less you have
to lower the bar and the more weight you can lift.

15

If your set-up is uncomfortable, that's normal. In fact, if you're not uncomfortable, your
set-up is probably not tight enough. Back stiffness and cramps in the lower body are
common when forcing yourself into the largest and tightest possible arch. A thorough
warm-up and some stretching generally help.

However, if you can cut a considerable amount of ROM, you may lose out on the
benefits of full ROM training for the bench press. If you're more than 8 weeks out from a
competition, it can be worth incorporating bench pressing with a more bodybuilding style
technique over the largest possible ROM to maximize muscle growth and overall
strength development. During the last 8 or so weeks, you should probably stick to your
competition style bench press technique to ensure you optimize that and you have a
good idea of your 1RM. You should also ensure you have at least one accessory
exercise for the bench press that allows full ROM training, such as dumbbell bench
presses or deficit push-ups.

Leg drive is essential to maximize strength for powerlifters, much more so than for
recreational lifters, because it can slightly help you push the bar off your chest and push
it back towards your shoulders after the mandatory pause on your chest. It also
provides stability and helps maintain a high arch. So experiment with what foot
positioning you can get the most stable set-up with and possibly press your chest up in
the bottom to 'launch' the bar off your chest, without your butt coming off the bench.
Your legs should essentially perform a leg extension against the floor.

Be sure to check if your federation allows a tucked foot position or if your heels have to
stay on the floor. IPF rules mandate your heels stay on the floor and your feet stay flat.
If you're not allowed to tuck your feet and stay on your toes, most people achieve the
best leg drive with their feet very far apart. Pressing into the ground should cause your
chest to pop up and your body to slide up on the bench when you're lying on the bench
without holding the barbell. This shouldn't actually happen during the press of course,

16

as your shoulder blades should drill you into the bench and the barbell helps pin you in
place.

Also check if your federation has a maximum allowable back arch. As of 2023, IPF rules
mandate that your elbows cannot stay above your shoulders. The bottom of the elbow
joint has to at least come in line with the top of the shoulder joint: see illustrations below.
This prevents only the most extreme of arching.

17

In contrast to most barbell lifts with high freedom of movement, the most efficient bar
path during the bench press is not vertical. You should press the bar up in a 'J-curve',
which means you press the bar up and back off your chest towards your face rather
than just up. The bar has to end up above your shoulders, and the bar's shoulder
extension moment arm is equal to the distance of the bar to your shoulder, so the
further away the bar is from your shoulders, the more work they have to do (together
with the upper pecs). Thus, it's best to get the bar above your shoulders quickly during
the ascent.

For the vast majority of people, the sticking point should be a few inches off the chest. If
you miss the lift before that, it's likely that your set-up is not tight enough and you're not
using leg drive fully yet or that your bar path is too vertical.

If you're wondering why we should lower the bar to our lower chest or upper abdomen
with tucked elbows in the first place then instead of performing a guillotine press straight
up and down, it's mainly because it helps you reduce the range of motion (and protect

18

your shoulders). You want to lower the bar to the highest point of your body, or close to
it.

If you fail the bench press near the top, that's also indicative of an improper bar path, as
biomechanically, virtually nobody should fail their press anymore once their elbows
have come significantly above their chest. All pressing muscles have good leverage at
this point.

19

The squat
The squat has to be performed to parallel depth. The definition of parallel varies per
federation. Under IPF rules, parallel depth is defined as: "the top surface of the legs at
the hip joint is lower than the top of the knees." In other words, you should be able to
draw a horizontal line from the top of the patella to the crease of the hip. Even if you can
go deeper, you shouldn't, as it will cost you a significant amount of weight on the bar.
Thus, the squat has to be practiced to exactly competition depth to ensure your lift gets
greenlit but you don't expend needless energy.

Parallel squat depth as per IPF rules

Again, however, if you're more than 8 weeks out from a competition, you should
probably incorporate full ROM squatting in your program as well to maximize overall
strength and muscle gains. An effective way to achieve this is to perform low bar squats
to competition depth and front or high bar squats with full ROM.

Low bar squats should generally be your competition variant of the squat, because
practically everyone should be stronger with a low bar position than a high bar position
and a stance wider than parallel. This popular powerlifting squat technique reduces the

20

external moment arms in the sticking point for the intervertebral discs. The lower the bar
is on your back and the more upright you can stay, the less work your spinal erectors
will have to do. You should experiment with the exact stance, as the optimum for
performance and comfort vary significantly between persons. Note that you may have
the most strength potential with a wider stance than you're comfortable with, especially
if you're not used to squatting wide, which will then at first feel very stiff. Just like for the
arch in the bench press, there's a trade-off between comfort and maximum
performance.

Your grip on the bar should be as narrow as your joints somewhat comfortably allow.
The more narrow your grip, the bigger the shelf your back muscles form and the tighter
you can pull the bar into your back. The more stable the bar is positioned on your back
and the tighter you can pull it into your body, the better the force transfer.

The sticking point in the squat is about midway during the ascent. If you fail the lift
below this point, you probably weren't tight enough and you need to focus on
maintaining muscular tension, especially in the core, throughout the movement instead
of divebombing down. Alternatively, your quads are too weak.

A common reason people fail in the sticking point is because they end up with a
'squatmorning' caused by rising their hips up faster than their knees. This does not let
your quads contribute fully and shifts most of the work to your posterior chain. If you find
this happening, you should think of leading from the chest of driving the hips forward as
you explode out of the hole. You'll likely also benefit from more quad work, as the
squatmorning is often a response to weak quads: when the quads fail, you intuitively
shift to a more hip-dominant execution.

21

The deadlift
The deadlift should be performed from the floor in competition, so you should do so in
training as well. In fact, powerlifting deadlifts should generally be trained exclusively with
cluster sets where you stand up in between reps to ensure each rep is biomechanically
similar to a competition lift.

Your arms should hang straight down, as close to vertical as possible, to minimize
range of motion.

Your feet can be placed inside your hands (conventional) or outside your hands (sumo
stance). A study by Cholewa et al. (2019), co-authored by Menno, found that most
people unaccustomed to deadlifting are equally strong with a conventional and a sumo
stance. There was a minor trend for people with longer torsos to be stronger in the
sumo deadlift, but everyone should experiment with both styles to see what they're
strongest with. Take into account your training history. If your sumo deadlift is already
equally strong as your conventional deadlift while you've always deadlifted
conventionally, for example, that's a strong indication your maximum strength potential
is greater with a sumo stance.

Your back position represents a compromise between performance and injury risk. To
minimize shearing forces and injury risk, an anatomically neutral spinal alignment is
likely optimal, as you learned in the Injuries module. However, most people are stronger
in a position of spinal flexion. Flexing the spine increases how much weight you can
deadlift in multiple ways:
• It reduces the barbell's moment arm on the hip, reducing the hip flexion moment.
• It reduces how much you have to flex the hips to grab the bar, putting the hip
extensors in a better length-tension position (closer to anatomical position) and
reducing their required range of motion.

22

• It stretches the erector spinae slightly, putting them in a better length-tension
position, mainly via passive force.
• It increases the passive contribution of the spinal connective tissues.

Most powerlifters have found that the best compromise between back health and
performance is achieved with a flat lumbar spine and a considerably flexed upper spine,
with no visible spinal movement during the lift until lock-out at the top. See Ed Coan's
deadlift below for a good demonstration of this deadlift form.

Powerlifting legend Ed Coan demonstrating spinal alignment that provides a good
compromise between maximal performance and injury risk. Notice how most spinal
flexion occurs in the thoracic spine, while the lumbar spine stays flat and the pelvis
stays in relatively neutral alignment (no major buttwink).

The sticking point is more variable for the deadlift than the other powerlifts. Most
strength trainees don't have a deadlift sticking point [2, 3]. Or rather, the sticking point is

23

right on the floor, which makes sense, because most people can produce far less force
from the floor than they can produce once the bar has passed the knee.
Correspondingly, mid-shin force production is significantly more correlated with 1RM
deadlift strength than mid-thigh force production. Thus, most lifters can either deadlift
the bar all the way up to lock-out or they can't get the bar off the floor at all. (Note that
some studies talk about a sticking region or sticking point defined as the point where
velocity decreases, but this is not a dead stop or the point where the lifters fail the rep,
so it's not a true sticking point.) In contrast, some powerlifters have a sticking point
around when the bar has to pass the knee, at least when they could get the bar off the
floor in the first place. No study has managed to find what makes some lifters have a
sticking point in the deadlift. The sticking point, if it occurs, seems to occur at a similar
place during conventional and sumo deadlifts [2] and in weaker and stronger lifters.
Theoretically, deadlifting with a rounded back, as powerlifters generally do, may make it
more likely to fail a deadlift higher up compared to right off the floor, as it makes you get
into a more favorable starting position, but then later on you have to extend the spine to
stand up straight.

To avoid a sticking point on the floor, get extremely tight before you pull the bar off the
floor. It helps to try to pull the slack out of the bar, or think of pulling yourself into the
bar, before you pull the bar off the floor. By already pulling hard before you initiate the
movement, you ensure you stay tight during the lift. Not starting tight enough is a
common mistake that causes the hips to shoot up and the back to round, leaving you in
a biomechanically disadvantageous and injurious position to start the movement.

Just like for the bench press and the squat, your competition technique is probably
suboptimal for overall strength development and muscle growth due to its relatively
short ROM. There is currently no research on the effect of ROM on ROM-specific
deadlift performance, but since full ROM bench pressing and squatting yields the
highest strength development over any ROM (see exercise selection module), it's likely
that the same applies to the deadlift, especially if your sticking point is at the floor. Thus,

24

when you're more than 12 weeks out, you may benefit from deadlifting with maximal
rather than minimal ROM. The best way to increase deadlift ROM is by performing your
deadlifts from a platform or foot elevation of sorts, such as a sturdy step or flat weight
plates, as illustrated below.

Deficit deadlifts

If you deadlift with a conventional stance and you have no way to implement a deficit,
the easiest way to increase ROM is by using a very wide grip AKA Snatch grip,
illustrated below. However, a Snatch grip is very difficult to hold without straps and it
makes a mixed grip difficult for many people, so you may have to try a Hook grip or
resort to powerlifting straps (see the course module on training gear).

Snatch grip deadlifts. With a wide enough grip, at some point your deadlift will start to
resemble a squat if you're flexible enough.

25

You can also increase ROM slightly by deadlifting with your spine in neutral posture, as
opposed to a flexed position. This also reduces spinal injury risk, but it significantly
changes your lifting technique, so it's generally best reserved for when you're more than
8 weeks away from any competitions. The last ~12 weeks, most lifters should primarily
train with their competition technique to ingrain this motor pattern and get a good idea of
your competition 1RM strength.

26

Repetition tempo
The general repetition tempo advice for strength training applies to the accessory lifts
for powerlifters: maximally accelerate through the concentric phase, slow down the
eccentric phase only as needed to retain control of the movement and then make use of
the stretch-shortening cycle to explode back into the concentric contraction.

The powerlifts also follow this advice, with 2 exceptions.
1. The bench press has to be paused on the chest, so you'll have to train for this
and you can't make use of the stretch-shortening cycle.
2. The deadlift has to be performed from a dead stop, so there's only a concentric
phase.

Tertiary mass building exercises follow the repetition tempo advice for maximum muscle
growth: aim for maximal repetition performance with the same controlled eccentric as
for accessory lifts.

Proximity to failure
Powerlifters should follow the regular strength training advice to decide how closely to
train to failure: generally 1-3 reps to failure for the powerlifts and accessory lifts and 1
rep to failure for mass builders.

27

Rest intervals
Powerlifters should ideally rest until complete recovery in between sets of the powerlifts
and accessory lifts, and at least until maximal readiness to perform. Combined with a
large number of sets for these exercises due to a low volume per set (e.g. 8 sets of 2
reps), powerlifting workouts generally take considerably longer than bodybuilding type
workouts: 1.5 to 3 hours is common for hardcore lifters. If time is an issue, making use
of the techniques in the time-saving course guide, especially for tertiary exercises, can
help constrain the workout time to levels closer to bodybuilding type workouts, which
generally shouldn't need to last longer than 90 minutes.

Exercise order
Since the powerlifts are always the highest priority exercises for a powerlifter, they
should normally be trained first every workout. Accessory exercises should follow and
tertiary mass building exercises should be done last. Since you'll have lots of long rest
intervals, you can save a lot of time using combo sets, ideally antagonist supersets, and
as long as there's no overlapping musculature involved and there's enough rest, it's fine
to break the general rule of performing the powerlifts first, accessory exercises second
and mass builders third here. For example, you can pair up the bench press with chin-
ups, the deadlift with dumbbell bench presses and you can generally even pair up
squats with leg curls.

28

Advanced training techniques
The following advanced techniques are useful for powerlifters.
• Reverse pyramiding accessory and mass building exercises to ensure there's
enough volume in the program for maximum muscle growth. It's generally better
to maintain a high intensity for the powerlifts and get additional volume from
different, safer exercises, if needed.
• Cluster sets for the powerlifts and accessory exercises, especially deadlifts from
the floor and breathing squats.
• Eccentric overloading, particularly for mass building exercises.
• Post-activation potentiation for 1RMs in the powerlifts.
• Low-load KAATSU training is ideal for mass building exercises to minimize injury
risk.

Motor imaging training
If you believe in it, you can do it. Well, maybe. At least you can actually increase your
strength on an exercise by intensely imagining yourself do it. The key to make it work is
specificity: imagine which cues you go through during the lift, how it feels and where the
sticking point is as if you're replaying an internal first-person video in your head. This
'fantasy training' is called motor imaging training. Your brain's motor cortex can to some
extent use this imaginary training to optimize its neural networks, similar to what
happens when you sleep.

While's it's cool that it works, if you can lift weights for real, not just in your head, motor
imaging training does not further improve strength development. The actual practice
makes imagining redundant. Still, motor imaging training can be useful when you're
injured or for some reason cannot train. If you're traveling and train in a crappy hotel
gym, for example, you can do a bodyweight workout for muscle growth combined with
motor imaging training for the powerlifts to make the most out of it. In case you want to

29

get serious with motor imaging training and visualization techniques for powerlifting in
general, this review by Alexander et al. (2019) (alt link) provides good guidelines.

Periodization
Powerlifters should aim for the fastest possible progressive overload, just like people
training for maximum muscle growth, so the same general periodization principles
apply: autoregulate your training volume, implement reactive deloads and make liberal
use of daily undulating periodization.

One particular aspect to pay attention to is planning your powerlifts on days when you
have the lowest fatigue. Muscle damage impairs motor learning, so it's best to train your
powerlifts when you're most recovered to optimize your technique. For example, if you
have split squats and powerlifting squats in your program, you probably want to do the
split squats either after the powerlift squat on the same or next day but not the day
before, as it typically induces significant muscle damage, which may impair your
powerlift squat technique.

30

Injury management
So far we've covered how to ideally set up powerlifting training programs for maximal
strength development. However, powerlifters often have to compromise on what's ideal
for strength for the neuromuscular system, because their connective tissues cannot
sustain the required training for it. This brings us to injury management.

As we've touched on several times in the course and this module, injuries present a
major obstacle for powerlifters. Injury rates in powerlifters are several times higher than
in bodybuilders [2], despite them typically not spending months dieting to contest shape
with compromised recovery. Practically all powerlifters experience minor injuries over
the course of their career and most experience one or several very serious ones. The
majority of powerlifters get injured at least once a year, usually mildly but occasionally
seriously. A considerable portion has to retire from the sport due to these injuries. This
is important to realize for all powerlifters.

The high injury risk also presents a programming dilemma. We know injury rates are
very high, but we also know most of the things needed for competitive powerlifting
increase injury risk, namely performing 3 movement patterns, 2 of which considerably
overlap, with a high volume, frequency and intensity with relatively little variation. While
it may be tempting to do everything optimally, if powerlifting is in the end just a hobby to
you, it may be prudent to program like the tortoise rather than the hare. That's to say,
consider doing the minimum effective volume rather than the maximum.

The minimum effective volume for powerlifters is a base of the 3 powerlifts, say
performing each twice a week for 3 sets each. You can generally safely program a good
variety of high-rep KAATSU mass building exercises on top of that. Depending on your
injury tolerance and progress, you can then consider adding more volume on the
powerlifts. Liberally program accessory lifts to substitute for the powerlifts if you don't
tolerate them at a higher volume. The powerlifts are relatively injurious exercises for
most people.

31

You'll soon know what your weak joints are, so program around those. Here are some
specific guidelines for the powerlifts.
• A narrow grip for back squats is optimal for strength, but the carry-over of wider
grip squatting to narrower grip squatting should be nearly 100%, so if a narrow
grip troubles your elbows or shoulders, go wider and possibly even overhand for
anything other than your 1RMs.
• Wide grip benching is hard on the shoulders. Consider spending some of your
time benching with a more narrow grip and using push-up variations and
standing or kneeling cable chest presses as accessory movements. They allow
the scapulae to move freely, which keeps your shoulders happier.
Also consider minimizing overhead work, especially with a barbell. It's also hard
on the shoulders and overhead strength is not a component of powerlifting, so it
has a low priority.
• Deadlifts are hard on, well, everything, but especially your spine and especially
when you lift with non-neutral posture, as most people have to for maximal
performance. Of the 3 powerlifts, they're also the least effective mass builders
and they're not nearly as technical as squats, so these are typically the first
powerlift you can consider cutting volume on. Many powerlifters deadlift just once
a week.

32

Nutrition & lifestyle
There is no difference in the ideal diet and lifestyle for muscle growth and strength
training.

Biorhythm management
As you learned, strength is time-of-day specific based on our biorhythm. Since a
powerlifting competition takes place on a certain time, you should start training at this
time to optimize your biorhythm for it at least 2 weeks in advance, i.e. if your show is in
the morning, start training in the morning 2 weeks out.

If you need to travel across time zones for your competition, you should ideally arrive a
minimum of 1 day in advance for each hour of time difference to let your biorhythm
adjust. Sunlight exposure, pre-workout caffeine and pre-bed melatonin
supplementation, as well as all the regular lifestyle and sleep optimization advice, can
considerably speed up the process and reduce strength loss from jetlag.

33

Supplementation
You can generally continue your regular supplementation protocol before a meet. The
only major exception is that most powerlifters should supplement a considerable
amount of caffeine before their meet. Since caffeine may become physiologically
ergogenic for 1RM strength at dosages above 3 mg/kg, caffeine is arguably the best
and only supplement that can acutely improve your powerlifting score. The benefits are
highly individual and not overly impressive. In many studies and even some meta-
analyses, 1RM strength does not significantly improve with caffeine supplementation.
Any benefits may be psychological or from offsetting negative biorhythm effects from
training at an unaccustomed time. However, the average 1RM strength increase in the
literature is in the range of 2-4% above placebo for the squat and bench press (few data
on the deadlift), which is very practically significant for high level powerlifting.

As such, if you're a caffeine responder and you don't get overly anxious from it,
supplement 3-6 mg/kg anhydrous caffeine on an empty stomach 1 hour before your first
expected heavy attempt in the meet. There is little dose-response effect and major
interindividual variability in the response to a given dose, so go with a dosage in this
range you feel good on, no more unless you've tried it before. If your competition lasts
several hours and you feel the effect of the caffeine waning, you can take a second or
even third dosage.

If you're very caffeine sensitive, make sure that you've consumed the dosage you're
going to take before for a mock meet. People prone to anxiety from high caffeine doses
may experience trouble focusing on their exercise technique when overstimulated.

After the meet it's prudent to take 5-9 days off caffeine consumption to prevent
tolerance and addiction.

34

Selecting a weight class & making weight
Which weight class is best for you? It's typically not the one your absolute strength is
highest in. How do you make weight? We discuss this in the following lecture.

➢ Lecture
Making weight for powerlifting [slides]

An additional semi-valid strategy to make weight beyond those discussed in the lecture
is a low-fiber diet. Reducing your dietary fiber intake for 1-3 days can significantly
reduce your body mass. However, it can also backfire. Constipation can obviously
increase your body mass. Most of the benefits of fiber reduction are likely attributable to
a decrease in FODMAP intake, so any further reduction in fiber intake poses
considerable risk for a dubious benefit.
headings: Selecting a weight class & making weight

pages.text:
35

Tapering
Before a powerlifting competition, many powerlifters program a tapering period. A taper
is a period before a competition where training volume is significantly reduced, or
training is ceased altogether, to let any lingering fatigue dissipate and thereby increase
performance.

Most tapering practices are largely based on intuition rather than science and the
tapering practices of powerlifters vary wildly [2]. The most common theory cited to
rationalize tapering is the fitness-fatigue theory of fitness, illustrated below, which states
that 'fatigue masks fitness' and hypothesizes athletes may accumulate 'low grade
fatigue' that's masking their true fitness.

While it's nice to think that we always have more in us, there's very little scientific
support for the idea 'fatigue' takes longer to dissipate after training than it takes to build
'fitness'. If we look at the sources of neuromuscular fatigue that can reduce force output,
none should last longer than the period of 'fitness supercompensation', which is largely
driven by muscle protein synthesis for muscle hypertrophy and neural remodeling. As
you've learned, the period of muscle growth after a workout, coinciding with the anabolic
window, can last for over 72 hours, at least in untrained individuals, whereas most
sources of neuromuscular fatigue dissipate much earlier than that.

36

• Substrate depletion recovers within seconds to minutes (ATP-CP system),
minutes (cardiorespiratory system) or at most up to a day (glycogen
resynthesis).
• Metabolic stress recovers within minutes up to an hour (e.g. lactate removal).
• Central nervous system fatigue normally recovers within seconds to minutes for
powerlifting type training.
• Muscle damage recovery is characterized by increased muscle protein
breakdown, which lasts shorter than the period of increased muscle protein
synthesis.

Of the above, protein balance seems to correspond best with the time it takes for full
neuromuscular recovery. This makes perfect sense in terms of General Adaptation
Theory, illustrated below: after a training session, supercompensation takes place
primarily after recovery. The initial hours after a workout are characterized largely by
recovery, while protein synthesis is still rising. After that protein synthesis and
breakdown both rise for many hours until net protein synthesis occurs and
supercompensation can take place.

37

The goal of a taper is thus to place an individual at the end of the supercompensation
phase for peak performance. At the end of the supercompensation phase is also where
you want to be before each regular training session, so the consideration for how long
to taper is theoretically the same as the consideration of how long to rest in between
workouts of the same muscle.

While many powerlifters err on the side of longer tapers due to fear of fatigue, excessive
tapers can be detrimental for 3 reasons.
1. You may lose relative gains by spending time on a taper that you could otherwise
have spent on productive training sessions. Gibala et al. (1994) compared 2 10-
day tapers after a serious arm training program in trained men: no training or
continuing maximal effort training with a progressive reduction in training set
volume. Performance was measured every 2 days of the taper. During the 10
days off, no significant performance changes occurred, though elbow flexor
maximum voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) force production started non-
significantly deteriorating after day 6. During the high-effort-but-reduced-volume

38

taper, performance kept increasing and peak performance occurred at day 10.
Statistical significance was not reached at all time-points, but the overall trend
was clearly in favor of continuing training with a high intensity but lower volume.
2. You may lose gains from detraining, especially after tapers lasting longer than a
week. Hortobágyi et al. (1993) found considerable albeit not consistently
statistically significant decreases in strength, muscle activity and muscle mass,
especially in the type II fibers, after 2 weeks without training in high-level power
athletes. Zaras et al. (2014) studied a super light 'speed work' taper with 30% of
1RM vs. a low-volume but high-intensity taper with 85% of 1RM for 2 weeks after
a season of training in competitive track-and-field throwers. The low-intensity
taper was clearly excessive, as it resulted in a decrease in 1RM leg press
strength, whereas performance improved significantly during the high-intensity
taper. Travis et al. (2021) found no
3. Several studies [2, 3, 4] find high-intensity lifting can potentiate strength
performance for up to 48 hours, at least for power and explosive lifting and only
in stronger lifters. It's unknown if powerlifting 1RM strength can be potentiated,
but more power could in theory help push through a sticking point.

There's also no need to taper gradually. Travis et al. (2021) found no significant
differences in powerlifting totals, total muscle size and Wilks scores between a 1-week
taper and a 3-week taper after an overreaching week with similar overall training
volumes during their 6-week programs. Type IIa muscle fiber size increased significantly
more after in the abrupt taper group though, suggesting the extended tapering blunted
size gains. There was a rough trend for the gradual taper to result in higher deadlift
strength, which tentatively supports deadlifts do better with more of a taper than the
squat and bench press. Other research found extended, gradual tapers may be
detrimental for the squat. Seppänen & Häkkinen (2022) found that after an overreaching
phase in trained men, squat strength improved significantly more after an abrupt 54%
volume reduction for 2 weeks than after a 38% volume reduction in week 1 followed by

39

another 32% volume reduction in week 2, bringing the total volume over time to the
same level.

Overall, these data suggest taking more than a week off before a competition is likely
directly detrimental and it's better to keep training at a high intensity but lower volume
than to take much time off in the first place. Indeed, Pritchard et al. (2019) found a trend
for better strength and power with a week-long taper with an increased intensity than a
decreased intensity in trained men. Both tapers involved a 70% reduction in training
volume. A 2007 meta-analysis on tapering for endurance training also found it's better
to increase than decrease training intensity during the taper, as long as total training
volume is reduced.

Ok, so a week is too long for most people, but how long should the taper generally be
then? As we discussed in the modules on training frequency and periodization,
neuromuscular recovery after even novel, high-volume exercise to failure typically
occurs within 24-72 hours in trained individuals. In support of this, Pritchard et al. (2018)
found no significant increases in either peak force production or the rate of force
production in trained men after taking either 3.5 or 5.5 days off after a powerlifting
program. There was a non-significant trend for performance to improve from pre- to
post-taper, but it didn't reach statistical significance in either group and there was no
notable difference in the improvement between groups. There was also no significant
change from pre- to post-taper in either group in bodyweight, EMG muscle activity, the
testosterone-to-cortisol ratio or creatine kinase levels, a marker of muscle damage.
However, countermovement jump (CMJ) height significantly improved after the 3.5-day
taper but not the 5.5-day taper, possibly due to the loss of the potentiating effect of prior
training after the longer taper. A similar study by Travis et al. (2021) found no significant
differences between a 3-day and a 5-day taper without any training after a powerlifting
program. Neither taper significantly improved performance. In fact, isometric bench
press strength slightly but significantly decreased after the 5 days off training but not
after 3 days, suggesting some detraining occurred after 3 days off. These data suggest

40

most powerlifters don't need to taper for more than 3 days before a competition and
tapering in general is unlikely to profoundly impact performance: theoretically, the gains
of a taper shouldn't be much larger than those from a single workout.

Weiss et al. (2003) compared taking 2, 3, 4 or 5 days off after a seated calf raise
program. The last calf raise workout was done for 4 heavy sets. There were few
significant differences between the different times off. Maximum calf raise torques didn't
significantly differ at either a slow or fast velocity between groups. For 1RM calf raise
strength, there was a trend for strength to peak during days 3 to 4 compared to days 2
and 5, but the trend only reached statistical significance for 2 comparisons: see the data
below. Prior to their 8-week calf raise program, the subjects in this study were
completely untrained. Since trained individuals should recover faster after a workout
than untrained individuals, this study suggests powerlifters should not take more than 4
days off before a competition.

41

Presumably realizing how artificial their study was, in 2004 Weiss et al. replicated their
study in well-trained men performing a complete upper body program focusing on the
bench press with considerably more volume. The last workout of the program consisted
of 9 1RM attempts in the bench press, 6 of which were performed in a novel isokinetic
machine. The last workout thus included novel exercise and eccentric overloading, 2
factors that majorly increase muscle damage and recovery times. Even after this heavy-
duty bench press workout, recovery seemed to be complete after 2 days, as after the
different times off of 2, 3, 4 or 5 days, there were no significant differences in 1RM
headings: of 9 1RM attempts in the bench press, 6 of which were performed in a novel isokinetic

pages.text:
42

bench press strength, either absolute strength or relative to bodyweight, or any measure
of force production or power. There was in fact a small effect size in favor of the 2-day
off period for both absolute and relative 1RM bench press. As you've learned earlier in
the course, other data indicate the bench press recovers at the same rate as the squat
and the deadlift, so these results suggest the ideal taper length for powerlifters is 2
days, possibly less.

While most powerlifters likely do best on a taper of just 1-4 days, there's some evidence
certain lifters require longer tapers. One of the earliest studies on tapering by Häkkinen
et al. (1991) found no beneficial effect for force production or muscle activity of a 1-
week taper in a group of Finnish powerlifters. However, when the sample was split up
into the 5 stronger and 5 weaker lifters, maximal isometric force production and muscle
activity increased in the stronger lifters during the taper while they decreased in the
weaker lifters. Strength never increased significantly above baseline, so it still wasn't a
successful taper, but anecdotally, highly genetically gifted strength athletes, especially
sprinters, have been observed to require longer recovery times than more genetically
regular athletes, possibly due to more fast-twitch muscle fibers or other traits that allow
for very high force production at the expense of poor fatigue resistance. Given its small
sample size, lack of any comparison group and no overall effect of the taper in the
group as a whole, this study provides very little weight of evidence, but it's good to keep
in mind some lifters may need extraordinarily long recovery times, possibly the
strongest lifters.

The remaining literature on tapering is unfortunately of very low quality both in terms of
internal and external validity for powerlifters. Many studies do not employ control groups
or even any comparison groups, rendering the results largely anecdotal. Most studies
also use highly theoretical measurements of performance rather than measuring the
outcome of practical interest, 1RM strength in the powerlifts, or at least some measure
of maximum force production.

43

Practical application
The following template is useful as a taper for powerlifters.
• The week before a competition, cut out all exercises except the powerlifts from
the training program. The minor acute carry-over they have for the powerlifts is
probably not worth the fatigue they induce anymore at this point.
• Increase the intensity of the powerlifts to a minimum of 85% of 1RM or perform 5
reps maximum per set without any compensatory increase in training volume.
This type of training induces the least neuromuscular fatigue and the most acute
strength development.
• 3 Days out, perform a low-volume type workout for your program that includes
each powerlift, or at least the squat and the bench press. This workout may
include conservative 1RMs: just make sure you don't fail any reps.
• The 2 days before the competition, abstain from heavy training and perform only
a thorough warm-up in which you build up to an explosive single at 85% of 1RM
in each powerlift, or at least the squat and the bench press. This workout may
have a potentiating effect on power during the competition and it's good active
recovery.

The deadlift is optional in the last 3 days before the competition, because anecdotal
evidence suggests it's more likely to be harmful than helpful. It's not a technical
exercise, it can induce a lot of connective tissue damage, the squat already trains most
of its involved musculature and there's little research on the deadlift. So if you feel you
don't tolerate deadlifts very well, feel free to omit them from the last 3 days before the
competition and schedule some extra time in between the last deadlift workout and the
competition, up to a maximum of 7 days.

44

Selecting your weights during the meet
At the start of most meets, you must provide the weight for your first attempt on each
powerlift. After that, you typically have 2 more attempts for each powerlift. Selecting the
right weights is crucial to get the right 1RMs. For this, it's crucial to know what your
training 1RM is, which means you should test your 1RMs in training. You should
probably incorporate 1RMs in your training for the powerlifts anyway, but you should
also test your 1RMs in a competition like environment, which means you test all your
1RMs on the same day in the order of your competition, which is typically squat →
bench → deadlift. Your squat shouldn't affect your bench, your bench shouldn't affect
your deadlift and by then there's enough rest your squat also shouldn't affect your
deadlift much, but it's nevertheless good to verify your 1RMs in this order. It's advisable
to schedule a mock meet one or several weeks out from the competition.

Theoretically, you could just list your training 1RM as your first attempt and not do the
other 2 attempts, as you shouldn't get them. Physiological variation in strength from day
to day in an optimized lifestyle should be minimal. However, if you fail your training
1RM, you'll accumulate a lot of fatigue and if you then fail the second attempt too,
you're on the ropes for attempt 3 and you'll be forced to select a very conservative
weight, as there's virtually no chance of doing well on a competition if you get a score of
0 for one of the 3 powerlifts.

So the time-tested approach to weight selection is to start conservative and ramp up the
weight during your attempts, starting with a conservative opener. Your first attempt
should be ~90% of 1RM, a very conservative number that functions like a warm-up and
a safety measure so that you're sure you've made a lift.

For attempt 2 many people select something very close to their 1RM but not quite it.
This is suboptimal if you know your 1RMs well, because you'll induce fatigue that will
limit your third attempt, meaning you'll likely end up with a lower 1RM than you were
capable of. So attempt 2 should be your estimated true 1RM, which is probably at least
headings: Selecting your weights during the meet

pages.text:
45

~2.5% higher than your in-training 1RM because of the taper, high caffeine dosage,
high motivation and strength development since your last in-training 1RM.

Attempt 3 should be left in reserve: go for it if attempt 2 failed or if you think you can
surpass your estimated 1RM based on how easy attempt 2 felt. Especially for the squat,
don't do it if you think it's unlikely you can improve on attempt 2, as it will fatigue you for
the rest of the meet.

46

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do the following.
• Design effective powerlifting programs.
• Explain the key differences between training for muscle growth and powerlifting.
• Judge what constitutes legal powerlifting technique.
• Help a competitor prepare for an upcoming powerlifting meet.
